cluster_name: Senegal President Faye sacks PM Sonko, parliament speaker resigns
event_summary: Senegal's President Bassirou Diomaye Faye dismissed PM Ousmane Sonko on 22 May 2026 and dissolved the government; parliament speaker El Malick Ndiaye resigned two days later, opening a path for Sonko to seek the speakership in an assembly his Pastef party dominates.
tldr: The headline layer collapses a multi-dimensional constitutional crisis into two registers: Anglophone wire and establishment outlets narrate an event sequence — PM sacked, speaker quits — framed almost uniformly as a political feud or crisis deepening; pan-African and French-language outlets look past the institutional sequence to foreground divided public sentiment and youth disaffection. The legal dispute over Sonko's parliamentary eligibility, the debt-crisis economic backdrop, and the anti-colonial ideological stakes that animate body-text coverage are entirely absent from headlines across all twelve articles.

evidence.quote: steps down as political crisis deepens
sacks PM and former ally after months-long feud
la rupture entre Sonko et Diomaye semblait inévitable
parliament speaker quits two days after prime minister
Dakar residents react to sacking of Senegal's PM Sonko
Sonkomania: Senegal's ousted PM strikes a chord
opiniones divididas sobre destitución del primer ministro
la rupture entre président et Premier ministre divise

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only outlet to deploy 'Sonkomania' in a headline — treating Sonko's support base as a culturally named phenomenon rather than a political faction, a framing absent from all wire services and European outlets.

Sole headline to characterise the Faye-Sonko split as 'inévitable' — an analytical verdict implying structural cause rather than personal decision; all other outlets report the event without attribution of inevitability.

The only article whose headline ('Africa: All of Africa Today - May 25, 2026') provides zero frame signal on the Senegal story — a daily-digest title that renders this article invisible to headline-layer framing analysis.

bottom_line: Headline coverage locks onto the event sequence — PM sacked, speaker quits — framed as political crisis or feud by six of eight buckets; only pan-African and French-language state-TV outlets look past institutional drama to surface divided public sentiment, while the economic, legal, and anti-colonial dimensions central to body-text coverage are absent from every headline in the corpus.
